what_it_does: Defines a concrete empirical test to determine whether Obsidian preserves nested YAML frontmatter arrays (links: and history:) when a board file is edited and saved, and uses the result to decide whether a direct-write path (Option D) can remain under consideration.
observations: The file is a noticeboard block item with YAML frontmatter and a Markdown body.
It proposes opening a copy of a real board file in Obsidian, editing only the body, saving, and diffing.
It lists three specific checks: preservation of links:/history: arrays, scalar key reordering, and Obsidian Sync mobile round-trip.
It frames the test outcome as a fork that either keeps Option D alive or eliminates it in favor of routing all two-way writes through a custom serializer.
It explicitly marks Option D as unverified pending the test.
standouts: It turns a tooling assumption about Obsidian frontmatter fidelity into an empirical gate before choosing an architecture path.
It identifies nested arrays of objects as the likely failure mode for direct Obsidian writes.
It states a clear elimination condition: if Obsidian mangles nested frontmatter, every two-way path must use the project's serializer.
conventions: Uses the ledger item schema with id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, channel, links, created/updated timestamps, and history.
Markdown body is structured with a numbered checklist and a decision tree.
References another board item (item-80c37615) via a part_of link.
Uses option labels (B, D) that refer to prior architectural options not defined in this file.
concerns: The options B and D are referenced without local definition, so the file is not self-contained.
The test assumes a specific real file (item-0822c9fa.md) exists and is representative; this is not verified here.
No procedure is given for how to perform or record the diff, or who owns the test.
notes: This item is a pre-decision empirical checklist that gates whether Obsidian can be trusted to write canonical board files directly.
questions: What are the exact definitions of options B and D?
What is the project's serializer and watcher architecture referenced in option B?
Has the proposed test already been run, and if so, where is the result recorded?
What does 'P10' in the title refer to?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to define a discriminating technical check before choosing a direct-write path, and the file does exactly that; purpose matches content.
apparent_themes: Tooling trust boundaries between external editors and canonical project files.
intention_signals: The file is trying to prevent the project from committing to a direct Obsidian-write path until there is hard evidence that Obsidian will not corrupt the ledger's structured YAML, and to force writes through a controlled serializer if the evidence is negative.
novelty: It converts a vague worry about Obsidian's Properties UI into a specific, reproducible diff test with an explicit pass/fail consequence for an architectural option.
contribution: It contributes a reusable empirical gate and a clear elimination criterion for tooling decisions involving external editors and structured frontmatter.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard item that prescribes an Obsidian save/diff test on a board file's nested YAML arrays to decide whether a direct-write path remains viable or must be ruled out in favor of a serializer-mediated write path.
intention_for_embedding: To establish an empirical checkpoint that prevents premature adoption of a direct Obsidian editing path until frontmatter round-trip fidelity is proven.
